title: Smt. Lata Ashok Khandagale vs Smt. Kamal Changdeo Sonawane And Ors on 28 January, 2021
body: Smt. Lata Ashok Khandagale vs Smt. Kamal Changdeo Sonawane And Ors on 28 January, 2021 Author: Nitin W. Sambre Bench: Nitin W. Sambre (16) WP-3550-20.doc BDP-SPS Bharat IN THE HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE AT BOMBAY D. Pandit Digitally signed CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION by Bharat D. Pandit Date: 2021.02.04 14:58:46 +0530 WRIT PETITION NO. 3550 OF 2020 Smt. Lata Ashok Khandagale ..... Petitioner (Original Plaintiff) V/s Smt. Kamal Changdeo Sonawane and Others ...... Respondents. ----- (Original Defendants) Mr. Dilip Bodake for the Petitioners. Mr. Abhijeet Kulkarni i/b Mr. Amol Patil a/w Sachin Hande for the Respondents. ----- CORAM: NITIN W. SAMBRE, J. DATE: JANUARY 28, 2021 P.C.:- 1] This Petition is by the Plaintiff to RCS No. 141 of 2019 questioning the order passed below Exhibit-75 on 01/10/2020 by the Civil Judge, Junior Division, Khandla, District Satara. Petitioner was directed by the Trial Court to handover possession of the disputed house to the Respondents/Defendants within eight days. In the alternative, Defendants are given liberty to take possession of the house if required with the aid of police protection. 2] The facts necessary for deciding present Writ Petition are as 1/11 (16) WP-3550-20.doc under:- 3] Petitioner initiated RCS No. 141 of 2019, alleging that the suit property was ancestral property. She claims to have succeeded to the same through her deceased husband as her father-in-law was in possession of the same. Based on the aforesaid theory, prayer in the plaint speaks of declaration as owner of the suit property and injunction restraining the Respondents/Defendants from interfering with the possession of the Petitioner. An injunction is also sought restraining the Defendants from damaging the suit property. 4] In the said suit, the Petitioner moved an application for grant of temporary injunction thereby seeking restraint order against the Defendants from interfering with the possession of the Petitioner. The Trial Court vide order dated 18/11/2019 rejected the said application, which order was subsequently confirmed in appeal preferred by the present Petitioner. 5] Subsequent to the above, alleging dispossession, Respondents/Defendants moved an application-Exhibit-75 claiming 2/11 (16) WP-3550-20.doc relief of restoration of possession under Sections 94 and 151 of the Civil Procedure Code. The Petitioner/Plaintiff claimed to have dispossessed the Respondents after her prayer for grant of injunction was rejected. Vide order impugned, the learned Trial Court allowed the application-Exhibit-75. As such, this Petition. 6] Mr. Bodake, learned Counsel for the Petitioner while questioning the order impugned would urge that the provisions of Section 94 and 151 of CPC do not empower the Court, particularly Civil Court to grant an order of mandatory injunction against the Petitioner/Plaintiff in her own suit. While inviting attention of this Court to the very language of Section 94 , he would urge that as the claim put-forth in the suit preferred by the Petitioner is not connected with the issue of dispossession, powers ought not to have been exercised by the Civil Court directing restoration of possession. Apart from above, submissions are, in view of express provisions in the Statute Book seeking restoration of possession, the Court below was not justified in invoking the provisions of Section 151 of CPC. 7] Further submissions of Mr. Bodake are, issue of lawful 3/11 (16) WP-3550-20.doc possession of property can be decided only after appreciation of evidence of the rival parties and, at this stage, by issuing summary directions of restoration of suit premises to the Defendants, the Court has exercised jurisdiction which is not vested in it which has caused substantial prejudice to the Petitioner/Plaintiff. According to Mr. Bodake, the Court is required to be sensitive to the fact that there is pending suit between the Petitioner and Respondents/Defendants which was initiated for simpliciter declaration and injunction by the Respondents, in which application for temporary injunction is pending. As such, according to him, instead of issuing mandatory injunction in pending suit of the Petitioner, remedy should have been by moving an application under Section 94 read with Section 151 of CPC in the pending suit of the Respondents/Defendants. As such, drawing support from the judgment of this court in the matter of Dinkar Tippanhna Mirajkar vs. Bank of India and Others reported in 2002 (6) Bom.C.R. 535, he would urge that the provisions of Section 94 of CPC ought not to have been invoked. Apart from above, he has also drawn support from the judgment of the Apex Court in the matter of Meera Chauhan vs. Harsh Bishnoi & Anr reported in 2006 DGLS (SC) 1165 so as to claim that the Court while exercising powers under 4/11 (16) WP-3550-20.doc Section 94 of CPC cannot decide possession of the parties to the suit. 8] While countering the said submissions, learned Counsel for the Respondents would urge that illegal dispossession by Petitioner was established in proceedings under Section 94 read with Section 151 of CPC i.e. Application-Exhibit-75 based on the report of the Court Commissioner. He would then urge that the Court Commissioner was cross-examined by the Petitioner and as such, report so as to infer dispossession has been rightly relied on by the Court below. The learned Counsel then would urge that specific case has been established by the Respondents of dispossession which weighed before the Court in exercising power under Section 94 of Civil Procedure Code. That being so, he has sought dismissal. 9] Considered rival submissions. 10] With the assistance of respective Counsels, I have perused the pleadings and prayer made in the respective suits. 11] On 18/11/2019, in the suit preferred by the Petitioner, Trial 5/11 (16) WP-3550-20.doc Court has rejected prayer for injunction and while doing so, a specific finding is recorded on possession of the Respondents/Defendants over the suit property. Though the Petitioner has not produced copy of the order passed in appeal against the aforesaid order rejecting the prayer for injunction, from the pleadings of the respective parties it is apparent that prima facie finding of possession of Respondents over the suit property has been upheld by the lower appellate court. In the aforesaid background, if we consider the case of the Petitioner as narrated in the Plaint and the fact that the Petitioner has prayed that Respondents be restrained from carrying out any construction, the Court below was right in inferring that the Respondents/Defendants were in settled possession of the suit property that too by virtue of registered sale deed executed in their favour. The Petitioner claimed succession to the suit property by virtue of inheritance as, according to her, the property was inherited through her father-in-law. However, it is established from the factual matrix and documentary evidence on record as is reflected in the order of refusal of injunction that the father-in-law of the Petitioner who had title to the suit property has already transferred the same in favour of the Respondents. 6/11 (16) WP-3550-20.doc 12] As such, it can be inferred that there was no occasion for the Petitioner to be in settled possession of the suit property and the theory of dispossession as has been inferred by the Court below based on the report of the Court Commissioner appears to be justified. 13] Apart from above, it can be noticed that once it is established that the Respondents were in settled possession of the suit property by virtue of their title to the same, the Petitioner under the garb of pendency of suit ought not to have dispossessed the Respondents. In such an eventuality, Court is armed with power under Section 94 read with Section 151 of CPC to issue directions mandatory in nature by directing restoration of possession to the Respondents which appears to be the case of the Respondents. Support can be drawn from the judgment of this Court in the matter of Harishchandra Naryayan Maurya vs. Rajendraprasad Dargahi Varma reported in 1997(3) Mh.L.J. 437 so as to substantiate findings of the Trial Court as regards powers to direct restoration of possession. The observations made in para 11 of the said judgment support the view expressed by the learned Trial Court. Para 11 of the said judgment reads thus:- 7/11 (16) WP-3550-20.doc "11. However, present case is not a case where the defendant was seeking a temporary injunction against the plaintiff under Clauses (b) and (c) of Rule 1, Order 39 and Nanasaheb's case (supra) has no application wherein the plaintiff on mis-statement of facts or mis- representation of facts obtain a order of temporary injunction from the Court against the defendant and in the guise and garb of that injunction order seeks to dispossess the defendant and in fact dispossesses the defendant. In such situation, and once it is found that in the garb of Court's order; the plaintiff has taken law in his own hand and by use of his muscle and power dispossessed the defendant, the Court in exercise of its inherent power could always pass an order of restoration of possession to the defendant to meet the ends of justice. Such power of the Court could be traced in section 94 as well as section 151 of the Code of Civil Procedure. As observed by the Apex Court in Manohar Lal Chopra v. Rai Bahadur Rao Raja Seth HiraLal , AIR 1962 SC 527 that Order 39, Rules 1 and 2 is not exhaustive and the situations not covered by Order 39, Rules 1 and 2 of the Code of Civil Procedure could be met by passing suitable orders in fit cases under sections 94 and 151 of the Code of Civil Procedure. Of course, such power is sparingly used and cannot be claimed as a 8/11 (16) WP-3550-20.doc matter of right but in deserving cases and where it is imminently required, the Court is not powerless and may pass appropriate order of temporary injunction at the instance of defendant in the interest of justice. The Court cannot be mute and silent spectator to the illegal act and exercise of force by the plaintiff in dispossessing the defendant, in the garb of Court's order of ad-interim injunction and once the Court finds that the plaintiff has taken the law in his own hand and by abusing the process of Court has sought to dispossess the defendant, the Court may not only pass an appropriate order for restoration but also it becomes bounden duty of the Court to ensure that the defendant who has been dispossessed by force is restored back his possession. Such order by the Court may be an exercise of its inherent powers under section 151 of the Code of Civil Procedure or under section 94 of C.P.C. and need not be covered under Clause (b) and (c) of Rule 1, Order 39, of the Code of Civil Procedure . In this background and legal position, the order of the trial Court in my view is expedient and meets the ends of justice. The impugned order is also imminently just and proper warranting no interference by this Court." 14] In the aforesaid backdrop, if we appreciate the submissions made by Mr. Bodake by placing reliance on the judgment of this Court 9/11 (16) WP-3550-20.doc in Dinkar Tippanna Mirajkar cited supra, the law laid down therein hardly supports the case of the Petitioner particularly when dispute sought to be raised by the Petitioner/Plaintiff is straightaway connected to the issue of dispossession of the Respondents and as such the Court was justified in exercising powers under Section 94 of CPC. Apart from above, reliance placed by the learned Counsel for the Petitioner on the judgment in the matter of Meera Chauhan cited supra will be hardly of any assistance, particularly when prima facie findings are recorded by both the courts below on issue of possession, while dealing with the prayer for grant of injunction against the present Petitioner/Plaintiff. In the aforesaid backdrop, powers exercised by the learned Trial Court under Section 94 read with Section 151 of CPC thereby ordering restoration of possession to the Respondents/Defendants who were dispossessed under the garb of pendency of suit appears to be verymuch justified. No illegality could be noticed in the order impugned which warrants interference in the extraordinary jurisdiction. Petition as such fails and same stands dismissed. 15] It is clarified that the observations made by this Court shall not 10/11 (16) WP-3550-20.doc affect merits of the pending suit. 16] At this stage, Mr. Bodake, learned Counsel for the Petitioner submits that the order of status quo passed by this Court be continued for a period of six weeks, as in the event Petitioner intends to challenge this order, she will need some breathing time. 17] In the wake of observations made above, I see no case in favour of the Petitioner which warrants stay to this order or directions to be issued to the parties to maintain status quo. Apart from above, this Court is required to be sensitive to the fact that Trial Court has prescribed procedure to be adopted in the matter of restoration of possession in the order impugned which, in my opinion, is likely to take sometime. That being so, prayer is rejected. ( NITIN W. SAMBRE, J. ) 11/11
